questions.prompt: Three roles in one customer (solicitor, paralegal, support) need different signature contents. What's the right primary approach?
choices: Three separate signature templates with different layouts
One template using Fields elements that collapse blank fields, with role-specific data populated in the directory or custom attributes
One template with three Senders rules and three sets of duplicated content
Three Brand Kits, one per role
questions.explanation: Field separators automatically hide if the field on either side is blank, so a single template renders correctly across roles when the data lives in the directory and custom attributes. That makes future role changes a directory edit, not a template edit.

questions.prompt: Inside a Text element, typing the open-brace symbol opens a dropdown of available fields you can insert into the text.
questions.explanation: Pressing the open-brace key inside any text-based element opens the field dropdown, so you can mix prose with directory data without breaking the element apart.

questions.prompt: Folder rules are evaluated AFTER the rules on each individual signature inside the folder.
questions.explanation: Folder rules are evaluated before signature rules. If a folder Senders rule excludes a user, no signature inside that folder applies to them, regardless of what the signature's own rules say.

questions.prompt: A user matches the rules of three enabled signatures in the All Signatures list. What does Exclaimer do?
choices: Apply all three signatures, stacked
Apply only the first matching signature in processing order, then stop walking
Prompt the user to choose between them
Apply the most specific signature regardless of order
questions.explanation: Exclaimer applies the first signature whose rules match, in the order they appear in the All Signatures list, and stops. That's why processing order matters: more specific signatures must come first.

questions.prompt: Which of these signal that folder rules and signature rules are in conflict for a user? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The Tester reports the folder excluded the user before any signature inside was evaluated
Two enabled signatures have overlapping Senders sets
The user is in the folder's Senders set but excluded by a signature inside the folder's Exceptions
A signature inside the folder explicitly includes a user the folder Senders rule excludes
A folder is disabled but contains an enabled signature
questions.explanation: The named conflict patterns are folder excluding before signature evaluates, and a signature trying to include a user the folder already excluded. The other options describe normal behaviour, not conflicts.

questions.prompt: A customer requires 'no end-user override on outbound signature' and 'consistent signatures on iOS, Android, and Outlook on the Web'. Which deployment satisfies both?
choices: Client-side via the Outlook Add-in only
Server-side only
Client-side via the Signature Update Agent only
Combined client-side and server-side, with client-side leading
questions.explanation: Server-side is the only path that prevents user override, and it's the only path that covers iOS and Android. Combined puts client-side first on supported clients, which weakens the no-override requirement.

questions.prompt: Server-side can pick the shared-mailbox signature for a 'Send As' email from a shared mailbox.
questions.explanation: Server-side handles Send-on-Behalf with a portal-managed signature choice (the Send-on-Behalf toggle in Mail Flow). Send-As cannot be steered server-side; client-side via the Outlook Add-in is the documented path for shared-mailbox Send-As signatures.

questions.prompt: Why does an MSP keep the customer's logo in a Brand Kit rather than uploading it directly into each signature template?
choices: Brand Kit images load faster
Brand Kit assets are encrypted; uploaded images are not
Updating a Brand Kit asset propagates to every signature that references it; uploaded one-offs require individual edits
Plan tier requires Brand Kit usage
questions.explanation: The whole point of a Brand Kit is the inheritance. A logo refresh in the Kit updates every signature using it; one-off uploads do not, and the redesign becomes 30 separate edits.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid Brand Kit assets? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Two brand fonts plus fallbacks
Two brand colours
Logo, Icon, and Banner images
A meeting background image
A list of approved recipient domains
questions.explanation: Brand Kits hold fonts, colours, logo, icon, banner, meeting background, and disclaimer text. Approved recipient domains are signature Recipient rules, not a brand asset.

questions.prompt: If two enabled Campaigns have overlapping date ranges and matching rules for a sender, only the first matching Campaign applies; the second is silently skipped.
questions.explanation: Exclaimer applies only one Campaign per email and processes the first match. The second is invisible. A shared marketing calendar plus pre-flight Tester runs avoids this biting in production.

questions.prompt: Campaigns deploy fully on Gmail in both client-side and server-side modes.
questions.explanation: Gmail HTML limitations mean Campaigns cannot deploy client-side on Gmail; server-side deployment of Campaigns on Gmail is supported. Same constraint applies to Disclaimers.

questions.prompt: A customer wants 'a confidentiality footer on every external email regardless of role'. Which Exclaimer object is the right fit?
choices: The Disclaimer signature element inside each role's signature
A Brand Kit disclaimer text used by each signature
The standalone Disclaimers feature with recipient type set to External Only
A Campaign banner with a date range covering the whole year
questions.explanation: The standalone Disclaimers feature is exactly this case: independent of which signature applies, recipient-type configurable, plain text up to 5000 characters. It survives signature template changes and applies after whichever signature was selected.

questions.prompt: Put the four pre-ship Tester cases in the order described in the Intermediate course.
items: A user the change should affect, to an external recipient
The same user, to an internal recipient
A user the change should NOT affect, to an external recipient
A user covered only by the catch-all signature, to an external recipient
questions.explanation: Confirm the change took effect, confirm internal-recipient behaviour, confirm no collateral on other audiences, then confirm the catch-all still applies for users with no specific match. Ordered intentional checks beat ad-hoc ones every time.
